Course Title: Insider Risk with AI Tools in Professional Services
Category: Finance & Professional Services: Banking, Insurance, Accounting, Consulting, Legal, Real Estate
Description: Employees exposing or misusing sensitive data.
Prerequisites: Learners should have a basic familiarity with everyday workplace tools and a general awareness of how client or financial data is handled within their organisation, but no prior knowledge of AI or cybersecurity is required.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the key ways employees in professional services can expose or misuse sensitive data through AI tools, and explain the insider risk behaviours that organisations should recognise and address. You will also be able to apply practical strategies to reduce the likelihood of accidental or deliberate data misuse, helping you protect your clients, colleagues, and firm from the growing risks associated with AI in the workplace.

lessons.lesson title: Insider Risk with AI Tools in Professional Services
lessons.Description: This lesson explores the growing risk of employees inadvertently exposing or misusing sensitive data when using AI tools in professional services environments. As AI-powered applications become increasingly common in the workplace, it is essential to understand how well-intentioned actions can still lead to serious data breaches or compliance failures. We will look at practical, real-world scenarios where insider risk arises, including situations where staff share confidential client information with external AI platforms without realising the potential consequences. By the end of this lesson, you will have a clearer understanding of the behaviours and habits that create risk, and the straightforward steps you can take to protect your organisation and its clients.
lessons.content: Employees exposing or misusing sensitive data.

slides.subtitle: Understanding the risks employees introduce today
slides.slidecontent:
• AI tools are everywhere now
• Employees share sensitive data
• Risks often happen accidentally
• Organisations face real consequences
• This course explains what to watch for

slides.subtitle: Threats from within your organisation
slides.slidecontent:
• Current or former employees with legitimate access
• Accidental mistakes or deliberate actions
• Misuse of trusted system permissions
• Different from external cyberattacks
• Same serious outcomes regardless of intent

slides.subtitle: How AI tools create new exposure points
slides.slidecontent:
• AI tools store inputs and use them for training
• Personal AI accounts bypass workplace controls
• Data doesn't disappear after your session ends
• Public tools lack enterprise security features
• Information can be accessed by other users or third parties

slides.subtitle: What employees do that creates exposure
slides.slidecontent:
• Pasting client contracts or financial data into public AI chat tools
• Sharing colleague or customer details to 'help the AI understand context'
• Using personal AI accounts to bypass company-approved systems
• Uploading confidential documents for summaries or analysis
• Saving AI outputs containing sensitive data to personal drives

slides.subtitle: Spotting problems before they escalate
slides.slidecontent:
• Shadow IT usage of unapproved AI applications
• Dismissing AI tool warnings or security prompts
• Sharing login credentials with AI platforms
• Uploading files without checking sensitivity levels
• Colleagues asking you to keep AI usage 'between us'

slides.subtitle: How innocent actions create serious risks
slides.slidecontent:
• Fee earner pastes client data into free chatbot to speed up report writing
• Someone uploads financial documents 'just for a quick summary'
• Staff assume professional-looking tools are automatically secure
• Documents containing names, case numbers, or deal terms shared without review
• Personal AI accounts used because they're faster than approved systems

slides.subtitle: Practical steps to protect sensitive data
slides.slidecontent:
• Check before you paste anything - pause and assess sensitivity
• Anonymise client details by removing names and identifiers
• Use only firm-approved AI tools and platforms
• Read and follow your organisation's AI policy
• Ask your manager when unsure about tool appropriateness

slides.subtitle: Essential principles for safe AI use
slides.slidecontent:
• Never put client or colleague data into public AI tools
• Treat AI outputs as drafts requiring human review
• Report accidental data exposure to IT/compliance immediately
• Your firm's AI policy protects you and the business
• When in doubt, ask before using AI for sensitive work

scripts.script: Welcome to this course on insider risk and AI tools in professional services. Over the past couple of years, AI tools have become a normal part of working life. People are using them to draft emails, summarise documents, analyse data, and all sorts of other everyday tasks. But here's the thing: when employees use these tools, they often share sensitive or confidential information without really thinking about it. And in most cases, it's not deliberate. People are simply trying to get their work done more efficiently. The problem is that these well-intentioned actions can create very real risks for organisations, including data breaches, regulatory penalties, and serious damage to client trust. So throughout this course, we're going to look at exactly how these risks arise, what they look like in practice, and what you and your organisation can do to stay protected.

Let's start with the basics. What do we actually mean by insider risk? Unlike a cyberattack from an unknown external hacker, insider risk refers to threats that originate from within your own organisation. We're talking about people who already have legitimate access to your systems, data, and processes. That includes current employees, but also former staff who may still have active credentials or retained knowledge of how your systems work. It's really important to understand that insider risk isn't always malicious. Sometimes it's simply a well-meaning employee who makes a mistake, perhaps sharing a file with the wrong person or using an unapproved tool without realising the consequences. Other times, yes, it can be deliberate. But in both cases, the outcome can be the same: sensitive information ending up somewhere it absolutely shouldn't be.

AI tools introduce unique risks that traditional insider threat programs weren't designed to handle. Many AI platforms store your inputs and use them to improve their models, so the data doesn't just disappear after you've finished your session. When someone uses a personal AI account rather than an approved workplace tool, there are no enterprise security controls in place. The information you share could potentially be accessed by other users of the platform, or by third parties who have agreements with the AI provider. This is fundamentally different from traditional software, where your data typically stays within your organisation's control. With AI tools, once you've shared that information, you've essentially handed over control to an external system that may have very different security and privacy standards than your firm.

So what does risky AI behaviour actually look like? One of the most common mistakes is copying and pasting sensitive material like client contracts or financial records directly into a public AI chat tool. People often do this with good intentions, perhaps to get a quick summary or draft response, but the moment that data leaves your system, you've lost control of it. Another issue is sharing personal details about colleagues or customers to give the AI more context. Again, it feels helpful, but it's a serious data exposure risk. We also see people using their own personal AI accounts at work to get around the tools their company has approved. And then there's saving AI outputs that contain sensitive information onto personal or unsecured drives. These behaviours all bypass the security controls your organisation has put in place.

There are specific warning signs that suggest someone might be creating insider risk with AI tools. Shadow IT usage is a big one - when people use AI applications that haven't been approved or vetted by their organisation. When an AI tool itself flags a warning or limitation, some users simply dismiss it and carry on regardless. Some staff even share their login credentials with AI platforms, which creates serious security vulnerabilities. Another red flag is uploading files without first checking what sensitive information they might contain. And perhaps most concerning is when colleagues ask you to keep their AI usage 'between us' - that's often a sign they know they're doing something they shouldn't. Recognising these patterns is crucial for preventing incidents before they happen.

Let me give you some concrete examples of how innocent actions create serious risks. Picture a fee earner who's under pressure to finish a report quickly. They paste client data into a free chatbot to help with the drafting - no malicious intent, just trying to be efficient. Or someone who uploads a confidential financial document just to get a quick summary, not realising that document might now be stored on external servers. We also see people assuming a tool is safe simply because it looks polished and professional. Looking legitimate isn't the same as being secure. Another common scenario is documents containing client names, case numbers, or sensitive deal terms being shared without anyone stopping to check what information is actually being exposed. These aren't malicious acts, but the damage to client confidentiality is exactly the same.

So what can you actually do to prevent these risks? First, develop a simple habit: before you paste anything into an AI tool, pause for a moment and ask yourself whether that information is sensitive. If it is, anonymise it first by removing names, client references, or anything that could identify a person or organisation. Only use tools that your firm has officially approved - if you're not sure which ones are on the approved list, ask. Take some time to read your firm's AI policy if you haven't already. It will tell you exactly what is and isn't permitted in your specific workplace. And if you're ever unsure about whether it's appropriate to use an AI tool for a particular piece of work, just ask your manager first. A quick question now can save a much bigger problem later.

Let's bring everything together with the key principles you need to remember. The single most important habit to build is this: never put client or colleague information into a public AI tool. Full stop. Always treat whatever an AI produces as a starting point, not a finished product that's ready to share. If something does go wrong and data is accidentally shared somewhere it shouldn't be, tell your IT or compliance team straight away. The sooner they know, the sooner they can help. Remember that your organisation's AI policy isn't there to make your life difficult - it's genuinely there to protect you as well as the business. And when in doubt, always ask before using AI for sensitive work. These simple practices will help you harness the benefits of AI while keeping your clients, colleagues, and firm protected.

Who is this for: Accountants, Financial Advisors, Lawyers, Consultants, Insurance Agents, Real Estate Agents, Bank Employees, Audit Staff, Compliance Officers, Client Relationship Managers, Professional Services Staff
Team or Dept this is for: Compliance, Risk Management, Legal, Human Resources, Information Security, Operations, Client Services
